enrichment.short_description: AI-powered, voice-first beekeeping app for recording hive inspections, tracking apiaries, queens, and honey harvests.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hive-Pal is a beekeeping management tool built around voice-first hive inspections: a beekeeper records an audio note at the hive and the app transcribes it and drafts a structured inspection report using AI (built on open-source models such as faster-whisper and Ollama).

Beyond inspections, it covers apiary-level management (multiple sites with weather context), queen tracking across generations, and harvest logging for honey yields. It also ships a set of free browser-based calculators for syrup ratios, brood timelines, swarm management, and colony strength estimation.

The project is MIT-licensed and can be run entirely on a user's own hardware — a server, laptop, or Raspberry Pi via Docker — with no dependency on third-party cloud AI services, or used through a free cloud-hosted version maintained by the developers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hive-Pal is aimed at hobbyist and semi-professional beekeepers who want faster, more consistent inspection records than pen-and-paper notes, and who value keeping their apiary data on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Voice inspections
features.description: Record an audio note at the hive; the app transcribes it and drafts a structured inspection report automatically.

features.title: Apiary management
features.description: Organize multiple hive locations with site-specific notes and weather context.

features.title: Queen tracking
features.description: Monitor queen lineage, performance, and breeding records over time.

features.title: Harvest logging
features.description: Record honey production and yields across seasons per hive or apiary.

features.title: Free calculators
features.description: Browser-based tools for syrup ratios, brood timelines, swarm management, and colony strength estimation.

features.title: Self-hosted AI
features.description: Runs on open-source speech and language models (faster-whisper, Ollama), so transcription can happen without sending data to a third party.

pros: Free to self-host under an MIT license, or use a free cloud-hosted instance
Voice-first workflow is faster at the hive than typing notes
No third-party AI cloud dependency required in self-hosted mode
Includes practical calculators beyond core record-keeping
cons: Niche tool focused specifically on beekeeping, not general farm/agriculture management
Self-hosting AI transcription requires enough local compute (or a Raspberry Pi/small server) for decent performance
Younger project with a smaller community than established agriculture software

faqs.question: Is Hive-Pal free?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is MIT-licensed open-source software that can be self-hosted for free, and the developers also offer a free cloud-hosted version.

faqs.question: Do I need an internet connection for the AI transcription?
faqs.answer: In self-hosted mode, transcription runs locally on open-source models (faster-whisper, Ollama), so it does not require sending audio to a third-party cloud AI provider.

faqs.question: Can I run Hive-Pal on a Raspberry Pi?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project is designed to run via Docker on hardware ranging from a server or laptop to a Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Who is Hive-Pal built for?
faqs.answer: Hobbyist and professional beekeepers who want structured, searchable inspection records instead of paper notes.

use_cases.title: Hive inspection logging
use_cases.description: Record a voice memo during a hive check and get a structured inspection report without stopping to write notes.

use_cases.title: Multi-apiary tracking
use_cases.description: Track hive health, queens, and harvests across several apiary locations from one system.

features: Data stays on your own hardware

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hive-Pal is a free, open-source, voice-first beekeeping app for AI-assisted hive inspections, queen tracking, and honey harvest logs.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source web platform that handles membership, subscriptions, and administration for Community Supported Agriculture (Solawi) cooperatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: juntagrico is a web application built to reduce the administrative burden that Community Supported Agriculture (known as Solawi in German-speaking regions) organizations take on as they grow. It manages members, subscriptions, and the paperwork that comes with running a shared-harvest cooperative, so organizers can spend more time on the farm and less on spreadsheets.

The software is maintained on GitHub and developed collaboratively, with ongoing development steered by an associated non-profit association (Verein) of the same name. It is already in use by dozens of CSA organizations, mainly across Switzerland, Germany, and Liechtenstein.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: juntagrico is built for CSA/Solawi cooperatives — groups of members who jointly fund and share a farm's harvest — that need a shared system for member management, subscriptions, and coordination without hiring custom software development.

features.title: Member management
features.description: Track members, their subscriptions, and their participation in the cooperative.

features.title: Subscription administration
features.description: Manage harvest shares and subscription changes across a growing membership base.

features.title: Reduced admin overhead
features.description: Consolidates paperwork that CSA organizers previously tracked manually or in spreadsheets.

features.title: Community-driven development
features.description: Maintained openly on GitHub with development priorities shaped by the associated user association.

pros: Purpose-built for the CSA/Solawi organizational model rather than a generic tool adapted for it
Open source and self-hostable, avoiding vendor lock-in
Backed by an active user community of CSA organizations
Reduces the IT burden on volunteer-run cooperatives
cons: Documentation and community are primarily German-language, which limits accessibility outside German-speaking regions
Best suited to the specific CSA/Solawi model rather than general farm management
Self-hosting requires basic server/Docker knowledge; hosted options depend on third-party providers

faqs.question: Is juntagrico free to use?
faqs.answer: The software itself is open source and free to self-host. Some organizations opt for paid hosting instead of running their own server.

faqs.question: What kind of organizations use juntagrico?
faqs.answer: Community Supported Agriculture (CSA/Solawi) cooperatives, primarily in Switzerland, Germany, and Liechtenstein.

faqs.question: Where can I find the source code?
faqs.answer: juntagrico is developed openly on GitHub under the juntagrico organization.

use_cases.title: CSA member and subscription management
use_cases.description: Coordinate members, harvest shares, and subscription changes for a farm cooperative in one shared system.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: juntagrico is a free, open-source web platform for managing members and subscriptions in Community Supported Agriculture (Solawi) cooperatives.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source online marketplace platform that lets local food producers and community food hubs sell directly to customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Open Food Network is a full-featured, open-source ecommerce platform built specifically for farmers, food producers, and community food hubs to sell directly to customers online. It was started in 2012 in Australia by the Open Food Foundation, a non-profit registered that year to build open-source tools for fairer, more local food systems.

The project has since grown into an international network operating in around 20 countries, with locally-led instances (each often run by a local non-profit or cooperative) rather than a single centralized commercial company. Instances can be run on the shared global platform or self-hosted, since the underlying software is released as open source.

The platform supports several selling models beyond a simple online shop, including aggregated 'hub' shops where multiple producers sell under one delivery/pickup point, which is common in food hub and cooperative buying-group setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open Food Network is aimed at individual farmers and food producers who want to sell directly online, and at community food hubs, cooperatives, and buying groups that aggregate orders from several producers into one marketplace.

features.title: Producer storefronts
features.description: Individual farmers and producers can run their own online shop for direct sales to customers.

features.title: Food hub / aggregated selling
features.description: Multiple producers can sell together through a shared hub shop with combined delivery or pickup.

features.title: Order and inventory management
features.description: Producers manage products, stock, and incoming orders through an admin backend.

features.title: REST/JSON API
features.description: A documented API (with an API Handbook) lets developers integrate or build on top of the platform.

features.title: Locally-led global network
features.description: Country and regional instances are run by local non-profits and cooperatives worldwide, not a single central vendor.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: As open-source software, organizations can deploy and run their own instance instead of relying on an existing local network.

pros: Purpose-built for local food commerce rather than a generic ecommerce platform retrofitted for farmers
Open source with a real self-hosting path, avoiding platform lock-in
Supports both single-producer shops and multi-producer food hubs
Backed by an established international non-profit network with over a decade of operation
cons: Feature depth and support quality can vary between local country instances since each is independently run
Self-hosting requires developer resources; most producers instead join an existing local instance
Smaller ecosystem of themes/plugins compared to mainstream ecommerce platforms

faqs.question: Is Open Food Network free?
faqs.answer: The software is open source and free to self-host. Local Open Food Network instances (run by non-profits/cooperatives in each country) often charge producers a small commission or fee to fund operations, which varies by instance.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Open Food Network instead of joining a local instance?
faqs.answer: Yes, since the platform is open source, organizations can deploy their own instance instead of using an existing country network.

faqs.question: Does Open Food Network have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers a documented REST/JSON API and an API Handbook for developers.

faqs.question: Who runs Open Food Network?
faqs.answer: It was founded by the Open Food Foundation (Australia, 2012) and is now operated as a locally-led international network across roughly 20 countries.

use_cases.title: Direct-to-consumer farm sales
use_cases.description: A single farm sets up an online shop to sell produce directly to local customers.
use_cases.audience: Independent farmers and small food producers

use_cases.title: Food hub aggregation
use_cases.description: Multiple local producers sell together through one shared storefront with combined delivery or pickup logistics.
use_cases.audience: Community food hubs and buying cooperatives

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Open Food Network is a free, open-source marketplace platform letting farmers and food hubs sell directly to customers online.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source administration platform for CSA cooperatives, handling members, delivery planning, and invoicing for subscription farm boxes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenOlitor is a web-based administration platform for regional contract farming and Community Supported Agriculture (CSA/Solawi) operations. It covers the operational chain of a subscription-box farm: managing members, producers, and personnel; planning harvest deliveries; and generating invoices and processing payments.

The software is released under the AGPL v3 license with source code on GitHub, developed by an international team of contributors. It can be self-hosted for free, or organizations can use paid cloud hosting from the project maintainers, priced at roughly CHF 30/month for an existing Swiss cooperative or about €1 per member per month in Germany; pricing for brand-new cooperatives depends on the chosen infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenOlitor targets CSA initiatives, farmers, and agricultural cooperatives running subscription-based produce distribution, mainly across Switzerland, Germany, Austria, and Italy.

features.title: Member and personnel management
features.description: Track members, producers, staff, and member absences in one system.

features.title: Subscription administration
features.description: Manage harvest-share subscriptions and project configuration.

features.title: Delivery planning
features.description: Plan harvest distribution logistics and generate related orders.

features.title: Invoicing and payments
features.description: Create invoices and process payments for subscriptions and orders.

features.title: Member portal
features.description: Gives members self-service access alongside customizable reporting and PDF document storage.

pros: Purpose-built for CSA/subscription-box farm operations rather than adapted from generic e-commerce software
Open source (AGPL v3) with free self-hosting available
Covers the full operational chain: members, deliveries, and billing in one platform
Active international contributor base
cons: Documentation and community are primarily German-language
Self-hosting requires server management; hosted plans have real recurring costs
Niche fit for the specific CSA/regional contract farming model

faqs.question: Is OpenOlitor free?
faqs.answer: The software is free and open source (AGPL v3) to self-host. Cloud hosting through the project is a paid option, roughly CHF 30/month for an existing Swiss cooperative or about €1 per member/month in Germany.

faqs.question: What license is OpenOlitor released under?
faqs.answer: AGPL v3, with source code publicly available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who is OpenOlitor for?
faqs.answer: CSA (Community Supported Agriculture) cooperatives and regional contract farming operations, mainly in Switzerland, Germany, Austria, and Italy.

use_cases.title: CSA operations management
use_cases.description: Run member sign-ups, delivery planning, and invoicing for a subscription farm-box cooperative from one system.

features: No licensing cost, only server expenses

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenOlitor is an open-source AGPL platform for CSA cooperatives to manage members, delivery planning, and invoicing, free to self-host.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source event management system built at CERN for organizing conferences, meetings, and lectures with registration and scheduling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Indico is a feature-rich, open-source event management system originally built at CERN, the European Organization for Nuclear Research, and released under the MIT license. It covers the full lifecycle of organizing lectures, meetings, workshops, and conferences: planning, registration, room booking and scheduling, document upload and archival, paper/abstract review, and long-term event archiving.

It is used at large scale by CERN itself (reportedly handling hundreds of thousands of events annually) and by other international organizations such as the United Nations for conference and participant management, alongside many academic and research institutions running smaller events.
Indico is self-hosted and open source, with active development and a public demo sandbox available for evaluation before deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Indico suits research institutions, universities, standards bodies, and any organization running conferences, workshops, or recurring meetings that need registration, room booking, and paper/abstract review handled in one system.

features.title: Event lifecycle management
features.description: Plan, run, and archive lectures, meetings, workshops, and conferences in one system.

features.title: Registration and access control
features.description: Multi-level access control and attendee registration workflows.

features.title: Document management and archival
features.description: Upload, retrieve, and permanently archive event materials.

features.title: Paper/abstract review
features.description: Built-in call-for-papers and review workflows for academic-style events.

features.title: Room booking and scheduling
features.description: Manage room reservations and detailed event agendas.

features.title: REST/HTTP API
features.description: A documented HTTP API allows external tools to integrate with Indico events and data.

pros: Battle-tested at massive scale by CERN and other large institutions
Covers registration, scheduling, document archival, and paper review in one open-source system
MIT-licensed and self-hostable with no per-event or per-user licensing cost
Public demo sandbox available before committing to deployment
cons: Self-hosting and configuring a full-featured event system requires real sysadmin/DevOps effort
Feature depth aimed at conferences/institutions can be more than small teams need
No official commercial support channel; relies on community and GitHub issue tracking

faqs.question: Is Indico free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open-source software under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Who built Indico?
faqs.answer: It was originally developed at CERN and continues to be maintained as an open-source project with public development on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Indico have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides a documented HTTP API for integrating with external tools and systems.

faqs.question: Can I try Indico before installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, a public demo sandbox is available on the Indico website.

use_cases.title: Academic conference management
use_cases.description: Handle abstract submission, review, registration, and scheduling for a multi-day academic conference.

use_cases.title: Institutional meeting and room booking
use_cases.description: Coordinate recurring internal meetings, room bookings, and document archival across departments.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Indico is a free, open-source event management system from CERN for conferences, meetings, registration, and paper review.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source platform (Antragsgrün) for submitting, discussing, and managing motions, amendments, and resolutions at conventions and assemblies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Antragsgrün (marketed at motion.tools) is an open-source platform for managing the full motion lifecycle at conventions and assemblies: submission and publication of motions, resolutions, and candidacies; amendment proposals; commenting and discussion; automated print templates and export formats; and on-site tools like agenda administration, speaking lists, roll calls, and voting.

It is released as open source under the AGPL license and available on GitHub for free self-hosted installation, with extensive customization options (branding, submission policies, single sign-on). Organizations using it include the European Youth Forum, the German and European Green Party, Volt Europa, and the National Council of German Women's Organizations, reflecting its base of NGOs, political parties, and advocacy groups.

Beyond the free self-hosted option, the team behind the project offers free trial instances (available for at least three days without registration) and paid professional services — custom development, hosting, and feature work — billed hourly, with primary servers located in the EU.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Antragsgrün / motion.tools fits political parties, NGOs, advocacy groups, and membership associations that run assemblies or conventions where members submit and vote on motions and amendments.

features.title: Motion and resolution submission
features.description: Members submit motions, resolutions, or candidacies through the platform for review and publication.

features.title: Amendment and discussion tools
features.description: Structured amendment proposals with commenting and discussion threads.

features.title: Print and export templates
features.description: Automated formatting for printed agendas and multiple export formats.

features.title: On-site assembly tools
features.description: Agenda administration, speaking lists, roll calls, and voting for live conventions.

features.title: Customization and branding
features.description: Configurable layout, branding, submission policies, and single sign-on integration.

features.title: Privacy-focused hosting
features.description: No trackers and minimal data collection, with primary servers in the EU.

pros: Purpose-built for the motion/amendment workflow of political and membership organizations
Free, open-source (AGPL), self-hostable option with no licensing fee
Used in production by well-known European political and advocacy organizations
Privacy-conscious design with EU-based hosting for the managed offering
cons: Advanced customization, hosting, and support are paid professional services, not included in the free tier
Documentation and community skew toward German-speaking organizations
Designed around formal assembly/motion processes, less suited to informal use cases

faqs.question: Is Antragsgrün / motion.tools free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is open source (AGPL) and free to self-host. The team also offers paid hosting and custom development services.

faqs.question: Can I try it before self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, free trial instances are available for at least three days without requiring registration.

faqs.question: Where is data hosted for the managed service?
faqs.answer: Primary servers for the managed offering are located in the EU, and the platform is built to avoid trackers and unnecessary data collection.

faqs.question: Who uses this platform?
faqs.answer: Political parties and NGOs such as the European Youth Forum, the German and European Green Party, Volt Europa, and the National Council of German Women's Organizations.

use_cases.title: Party convention motion management
use_cases.description: Run submission, amendment, discussion, and voting on motions for a political party convention.
use_cases.audience: Political parties and their local chapters

use_cases.title: NGO/association assemblies
use_cases.description: Manage resolutions and candidacy submissions for a membership association's general assembly.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Antragsgrün (motion.tools) is a free, open-source platform for managing motions, amendments, and votes at conventions and assemblies.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, web-based system for digital motion management, online voting, and committee meetings, used by unions and parliaments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenSlides is a web-based system for digitally organizing meetings, assemblies, and committee structures. It handles online voting and secret elections, motion/proposal management for multi-stage approval processes, participant management with role-based access, agenda planning and speaker lists, file administration, and live video streaming, plus an 'Autopilot' mode for running sessions with less manual operation.

The software is Free/Libre Open Source Software released under the MIT license, self-hostable and free to run without licensing cost. Notable users include the German Trade Union Confederation (DGB), the SPD, the Greens, the FDP, and Amnesty International, reflecting adoption by unions, parties, works councils, and NGOs.

OpenSlides also sells managed hosting and services through its commercial arm, with a public price calculator for hosted deployments, and offers customization of its API for connecting external software plus video-conferencing integration for hybrid or fully virtual meetings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenSlides fits unions, political parties, works councils, parliaments, and membership associations that need legally-compliant online voting and structured motion management for their meetings and assemblies.

features.title: Online voting and elections
features.description: Run secret elections and online votes designed for legal compliance in formal assemblies.

features.title: Motion management
features.description: Track proposals and motions through multi-stage approval processes.

features.title: Participant and access management
features.description: Manage attendee roles and permissions with differentiated access controls.

features.title: Agenda and speaker lists
features.description: Plan meeting agendas and manage speaker queues in real time.

features.title: Live video streaming
features.description: Stream meetings live for hybrid or remote participants.

features.title: Autopilot mode
features.description: Automates routine session-management tasks during live meetings.

pros: Free, MIT-licensed, self-hostable software with no per-seat licensing cost
Used in production by major unions, parties, and international NGOs
Covers legally-compliant online voting alongside general meeting management
Optional managed hosting available for organizations that don't want to self-host
cons: Self-hosting the full stack (multiple backend services, datastore, Redis) requires real DevOps capacity
Advanced API customization and video integration are offered as paid professional services
Documentation and primary market skew German/European

faqs.question: Is OpenSlides free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is Free/Libre Open Source Software under the MIT license and can be self-hosted at no licensing cost. Managed hosting is available as a paid option.

faqs.question: Does OpenSlides support online voting?
faqs.answer: Yes, including secret elections designed to meet legal requirements for formal assemblies.

faqs.question: Who uses OpenSlides?
faqs.answer: Unions, political parties, works councils, and NGOs, including the German Trade Union Confederation (DGB), SPD, the Greens, FDP, and Amnesty International.

faqs.question: Can I integrate OpenSlides with other software?
faqs.answer: OpenSlides offers API customization services for connecting external software, in addition to its core self-hosted platform.

use_cases.title: Union and works council meetings
use_cases.description: Run motions, votes, and elections for a union or works council assembly with a legally-compliant digital process.

use_cases.title: Party conventions and elections
use_cases.description: Manage agendas, speaker lists, motions, and secret elections at a political party convention.

features: Optional API customization and video integration services

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenSlides is a free, open-source, MIT-licensed platform for digital motions, online voting, and committee meeting management.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source event management app tailored for free/open-source software conferences, covering call-for-papers, scheduling, and registration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OSEM (Open Source Event Manager) is an open-source event management app built specifically for free and open-source software conferences and community-driven tech events. It handles call-for-papers workflows (receiving, categorizing, evaluating, and scheduling speaker submissions), event marketing tools such as landing pages, agendas, and visitor registration with optional ticketing, and basic analytics on program development and audience engagement.

The software is entirely open source and self-hosted, with a public demo and downloadable releases on GitHub. It has been used by community events including openSUSE conferences, GNOME events, ownCloud events, PostgreSQL Conference, and LinuxFest Northwest.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OSEM is aimed at organizers of free/open-source software conferences and other community-run tech events who need call-for-papers, scheduling, and registration handled by software that is itself open source.

features.title: Call-for-papers management
features.description: Receive, categorize, evaluate, and schedule speaker submissions for a conference.

features.title: Event landing pages and agendas
features.description: Build public-facing pages describing the event and its schedule.

features.title: Visitor registration and ticketing
features.description: Handle attendee registration with optional ticketing.

features.title: Session detail pages
features.description: Dedicated pages for each talk/session with details for attendees.

features.title: Basic analytics
features.description: Insights into program development and audience engagement.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hosted with no licensing cost
Purpose-built for the FOSS conference call-for-papers and scheduling workflow
Proven in production by well-known open-source community events
Public demo available before committing to a deployment
cons: Feature set and design assume a community/FOSS-conference context, less suited to commercial event production
Community-maintained project without a commercial support option
Self-hosting requires developer/sysadmin effort to deploy and maintain

faqs.question: Is OSEM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is fully open-source and self-hosted software available at no cost.

faqs.question: What kind of events is OSEM designed for?
faqs.answer: Free and open-source software conferences and other community-driven technology events.

faqs.question: Which organizations have used OSEM?
faqs.answer: Community events including openSUSE, GNOME, ownCloud, PostgreSQL Conference, and LinuxFest Northwest.

faqs.question: Can I try OSEM before installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, a demo is available, and releases can be downloaded from GitHub.

use_cases.title: FOSS conference call-for-papers
use_cases.description: Collect, review, and schedule speaker submissions for an open-source software conference.

use_cases.title: Community event registration
use_cases.description: Publish an event landing page and handle attendee registration and optional ticketing.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OSEM is a free, open-source event management app for FOSS conferences, covering call-for-papers, scheduling, and registration.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source edition of Alfresco's enterprise content management platform for self-hosted document management and workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Alfresco Community Edition is the free, open-source edition of Alfresco's enterprise content services suite, now developed under Hyland (which acquired Alfresco). It provides document management, content repository, and basic process/workflow capabilities that can be self-hosted without a license fee.

The Community Edition is released under LGPL and remains under active development — recent numbered releases (23.x and 25.x series) have continued through 2025 — but it is distributed without official vendor support, unlike Alfresco's paid Enterprise/Content Services products which add governance, advanced process automation, official support, and enterprise integrations.

Source and releases are available via GitHub and SourceForge, and it is commonly used by developers and technical teams evaluating or building on Alfresco's content services architecture before deciding whether to adopt the commercial edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Alfresco Community Edition suits developers, technical teams, and organizations that want a free, self-hosted document/content management repository and are comfortable running it without official vendor support.

features.title: Document management repository
features.description: Store, version, and organize documents and other content in a central repository.

features.title: Access control
features.description: Manage permissions and access to content across users and groups.

features.title: Basic process/workflow
features.description: Route content through simple approval and review processes.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access to the content repository for integrations and custom applications.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs on your own infrastructure with no per-user licensing fee for the Community Edition.

pros: Free, open-source (LGPL) alternative to paid enterprise content management platforms
Mature, long-running project with continued releases into 2025
REST API supports custom integrations and application development
Self-hosted, keeping content data under the organization's own control
cons: No official vendor support for Community Edition, unlike Alfresco's paid Enterprise product
Advanced governance, process automation, and enterprise integrations are reserved for the commercial edition
Deployment and upgrades require in-house technical expertise

faqs.question: Is Alfresco Community Edition still available and maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, it continues to receive numbered releases (including 23.x and 25.x series through 2025) and is available on GitHub and SourceForge, though without official vendor support.

faqs.question: What license is Alfresco Community Edition released under?
faqs.answer: LGPL.

faqs.question: How does Community Edition differ from Alfresco's paid product?
faqs.answer: The paid Enterprise/Content Services product adds official vendor support, advanced governance, more process automation, and enterprise integrations that Community Edition does not include.

faqs.question: Does Alfresco Community Edition have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a REST API for building integrations and custom applications on top of the content repository.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted document management
use_cases.description: Deploy a free, self-hosted content repository for storing and version-controlling organizational documents.

use_cases.title: Evaluating Alfresco before enterprise adoption
use_cases.description: Test the core content services architecture on Community Edition before committing to the paid Enterprise product.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Alfresco Community Edition is the free, open-source, self-hosted edition of Alfresco's document and content management platform.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Node.js CMS with in-context visual editing and headless capabilities, offering a free tier plus paid Pro and hosting plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ApostropheCMS is an open-source content management system built on Node.js, MongoDB, and Vue.js, combining in-context visual editing for content teams with a modular, extensible architecture for developers. It can also run headless, powering front ends built with frameworks like Astro, Gatsby, or Nuxt.

The project traces back to Philadelphia web design firm P'unk Avenue, which spun Apostrophe out into its own company, Apostrophe Technologies, in 2019 after originally building the CMS to power its own client sites. The company remains headquartered in Philadelphia.

The core CMS (Community/Open Source Edition) is free and self-hosted, with unlimited users and content types. Paid tiers — Apostrophe Pro and Apostrophe Assembly — add advanced permissions, document version history, AI-assisted translation and SEO tools, multisite management, and white-label options, alongside optional managed hosting plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ApostropheCMS is used by digital agencies building client websites, software companies embedding a CMS into their own products, and enterprise teams managing multiple brand sites — with self-hosting for cost-sensitive teams and paid Pro/Assembly tiers for teams that want advanced editorial or multisite features plus support.

features.title: In-context visual editing
features.description: Content editors edit pages directly on the live-rendered site rather than through a separate form-based admin panel.

features.title: Headless CMS mode
features.description: Serve content as data to front ends built with Astro, Gatsby, Nuxt, or other frameworks.

features.title: Modular, extensible architecture
features.description: Developers extend or override core modules to customize functionality.

features.title: Advanced permissions and document history
features.description: Available in Pro: granular editor permissions and version history for content changes.

features.title: AI-assisted translation and SEO
features.description: Pro tier includes AI-powered translation and SEO tooling for content.

features.title: Multisite management (Assembly)
features.description: Manage many sites from a single codebase with a multitenant dashboard and shared templates.

pros: Free, open-source core edition with unlimited users and content types
In-context visual editing is friendlier for non-technical content teams than typical headless CMS admin panels
Can run traditional or headless depending on the front-end framework in use
Paid tiers add enterprise features (multisite, white-label, advanced permissions) without forking away from the open-source core
cons: Advanced permissions, version history, AI tools, and multisite management require paid Pro or Assembly plans
Built on Node.js/MongoDB, so teams need familiarity with that stack to self-host and extend it
Managed hosting is a separate paid add-on, not bundled into the free tier

faqs.question: Is ApostropheCMS free?
faqs.answer: The Open Source/Community Edition is free and self-hosted with unlimited users and content types. Apostrophe Pro starts at $199/month for 5 editors, and Apostrophe Assembly starts at $499/month for 5 sites.

faqs.question: Can ApostropheCMS run headless?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports headless deployment for front ends built with frameworks such as Astro, Gatsby, and Nuxt, in addition to its traditional in-context editing mode.

faqs.question: Who makes ApostropheCMS?
faqs.answer: Apostrophe Technologies, based in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, which spun out of web design firm P'unk Avenue in 2019.

faqs.question: Does Apostrophe offer managed hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, as a separate paid option, with Basic environments starting at $39/month and Performance environments at $89/app server.

use_cases.title: Agency client website builds
use_cases.description: Digital agencies use the open-source or Pro edition to build editable marketing sites for clients.

use_cases.title: Embedded CMS in a SaaS product
use_cases.description: Software companies integrate ApostropheCMS as the content layer inside their own product.

use_cases.title: Multi-brand enterprise sites
use_cases.description: Manage several brand or regional websites from one shared codebase using Apostrophe Assembly.

features: Localization, custom forms, blog, SEO tools, SSO

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ApostropheCMS is an open-source Node.js CMS with visual in-context editing, a free self-hosted tier, and paid Pro/Assembly plans.
